source_paper_title: Splitting Guarantees for Prophet Inequalities via Nonlinear Systems
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Brustle_2025, title={Splitting Guarantees for Prophet Inequalities via Nonlinear Systems}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2024.0413}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2024.0413}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Brustle, Johannes and Perez-Salazar, Sebastian and Verdugo, Victor}, year={2025}, month=apr }
context:
Section 6 (Final Remarks), page 28 (arXiv v2 pagination).

Finding an analytical formula for the approximation ratio of \((k, n)\)-SPI remains a problem, but our findings offer a potential avenue toward this goal.
title: Characterize the exact worst-case approximation ratio for i.i.d. k-selection prophet inequalities
background_latex:
Fix integers \(n\ge k\ge 1\). In the i.i.d. \(k\)-selection prophet inequality problem, a decision-maker observes a sequence of nonnegative random values \(X_1,\dots,X_n\) revealed sequentially, where \(X_t\) are independent and identically distributed from a known distribution \(F\) supported on \(\mathbb{R}_+\). After observing \(X_t\), the decision-maker must irrevocably either accept it (and then it contributes \(X_t\) to the payoff) or reject it. The policy may accept at most \(k\) values in total.

Let \(\pi\) denote any (possibly randomized) online policy for this problem. Its expected reward is \(\mathbb{E}[\sum_{t\in S_\pi} X_t]\), where \(S_\pi\subseteq\{1,\dots,n\}\) is the (random) set of accepted indices with \(|S_\pi|\le k\).

The offline (prophet) benchmark is the expected sum of the \(k\) largest values among \(X_1,\dots,X_n\). Writing the order statistics as \(X_{(1)}\le \cdots \le X_{(n)}\), the benchmark is
\[
\mathrm{OPT}_{n,k}(F)=\sum_{i=n-k+1}^n \mathbb{E}[X_{(i)}].
\]
The approximation ratio of a policy \(\pi\) on distribution \(F\) is
\[
\mathrm{AR}_{n,k}(\pi,F)=\frac{\mathbb{E}[\sum_{t\in S_\pi} X_t]}{\mathrm{OPT}_{n,k}(F)}.
\]
Define the optimal worst-case approximation ratio for \((k,n)\) as
\[
\gamma_{n,k}=\sup_{\pi}\;\inf_{F}\; \mathrm{AR}_{n,k}(\pi,F),
\]
where the infimum is over i.i.d. nonnegative distributions \(F\) (the paper works with continuous \(F\), but the precise regularity is not essential for stating the ratio).

For \(k=1\), \(\gamma_{n,1}\) has a known tight value in the limit \(n\to\infty\), obtained via the Hill--Kertz differential equation; for \(k\ge 2\), no comparable closed-form characterization is known. The paper introduces a coupled nonlinear system of differential equations, parameterized by constants \(\theta_1,\dots,\theta_k\), whose solvability yields provable lower bounds of the form \(\gamma_{n,k}\gtrsim \sum_{j=1}^k \theta_j\) for large \(n\), but it does not give an exact analytical formula for \(\gamma_{n,k}\) or for its worst-case limit over \(n\).
problem_definition_latex: Determine an analytical (closed-form) characterization of the worst-case optimal approximation ratio \(\gamma_{n,k}\) for the i.i.d. \((k,n)\)-selection prophet inequality problem, for general \(k\ge 2\) (and/or of the worst-case limit \(\inf_{n\ge 1}\gamma_{n,k}\)), rather than only bounds derived from the nonlinear differential-equation system.
latex:
Fix integers \(n\ge k\ge 1\). In the i.i.d. \(k\)-selection prophet inequality problem, a decision-maker observes a sequence of nonnegative random values \(X_1,\dots,X_n\) revealed sequentially, where \(X_t\) are independent and identically distributed from a known distribution \(F\) supported on \(\mathbb{R}_+\). After observing \(X_t\), the decision-maker must irrevocably either accept it (and then it contributes \(X_t\) to the payoff) or reject it. The policy may accept at most \(k\) values in total.

Let \(\pi\) denote any (possibly randomized) online policy for this problem. Its expected reward is \(\mathbb{E}[\sum_{t\in S_\pi} X_t]\), where \(S_\pi\subseteq\{1,\dots,n\}\) is the (random) set of accepted indices with \(|S_\pi|\le k\).

The offline (prophet) benchmark is the expected sum of the \(k\) largest values among \(X_1,\dots,X_n\). Writing the order statistics as \(X_{(1)}\le \cdots \le X_{(n)}\), the benchmark is
\[
\mathrm{OPT}_{n,k}(F)=\sum_{i=n-k+1}^n \mathbb{E}[X_{(i)}].
\]
The approximation ratio of a policy \(\pi\) on distribution \(F\) is
\[
\mathrm{AR}_{n,k}(\pi,F)=\frac{\mathbb{E}[\sum_{t\in S_\pi} X_t]}{\mathrm{OPT}_{n,k}(F)}.
\]
Define the optimal worst-case approximation ratio for \((k,n)\) as
\[
\gamma_{n,k}=\sup_{\pi}\;\inf_{F}\; \mathrm{AR}_{n,k}(\pi,F),
\]
where the infimum is over i.i.d. nonnegative distributions \(F\) (the paper works with continuous \(F\), but the precise regularity is not essential for stating the ratio).

For \(k=1\), \(\gamma_{n,1}\) has a known tight value in the limit \(n\to\infty\), obtained via the Hill--Kertz differential equation; for \(k\ge 2\), no comparable closed-form characterization is known. The paper introduces a coupled nonlinear system of differential equations, parameterized by constants \(\theta_1,\dots,\theta_k\), whose solvability yields provable lower bounds of the form \(\gamma_{n,k}\gtrsim \sum_{j=1}^k \theta_j\) for large \(n\), but it does not give an exact analytical formula for \(\gamma_{n,k}\) or for its worst-case limit over \(n\).

Determine an analytical (closed-form) characterization of the worst-case optimal approximation ratio \(\gamma_{n,k}\) for the i.i.d. \((k,n)\)-selection prophet inequality problem, for general \(k\ge 2\) (and/or of the worst-case limit \(\inf_{n\ge 1}\gamma_{n,k}\)), rather than only bounds derived from the nonlinear differential-equation system.
open_problem_quote: Finding an analytical formula for the approximation ratio of \((k, n)\)-SPI remains a problem, but our findings offer a potential avenue toward this goal.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question asks for an analytical formula/characterization rather than a yes/no conjecture on a concrete finite structure, so there is no clear finite counterexample space to search. Numerical experiments can suggest formulas but cannot certify them as exact across all distributions and all \(n\).
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can algorithmically compute \(\gamma_{n,k}\) numerically via the paper’s LP formulations and attempt to fit candidate closed forms or identify structural patterns (e.g., via solving the nonlinear ODE system and matching to LP optima) with an automatic fitness signal (gap between candidate formula and computed values). However, turning a fitted expression into a correct analytical characterization still requires substantial proof beyond computational search.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The open problem from Brustle--Perez-Salazar--Verdugo ("Splitting Guarantees for Prophet Inequalities via Nonlinear Systems") asks for an analytic closed-form characterization of the minimax i.i.d. \((k,n)\)-selection prophet ratio \(\gamma_{n,k}=\sup_\pi\inf_F \mathrm{AR}_{n,k}(\pi,F)\) for \(k\ge 2\), analogous to the Hill--Kertz ODE characterization for \(k=1\). The source paper itself makes two key structural advances: it gives an exact infinite-dimensional quantile-space LP \([P]_{n,k}\) for \(\gamma_{n,k}\) (with the worst-case distribution encoded by a nonincreasing quantile function \(h(u)=F^{-1}(1-u)\)), and it derives a coupled nonlinear ODE system (involving upper incomplete gamma functions \(\Gamma_\ell\)) whose solvability at parameters \(\theta_1^*,\dots,\theta_k^*\) yields asymptotic lower bounds \(\gamma_{n,k}\gtrsim \sum_{j=1}^k \theta_j^*\) for large \(n\). Numerically, this gives strong constants (e.g. \(\sum_{j=1}^2\theta_j^*\approx 0.829\)), but the paper explicitly leaves open whether \(\inf_n\gamma_{n,k}=\sum_{j=1}^k\theta_j^*\) or any closed-form expression exists.

Subsequent work provides complementary partial progress toward “exactness,” but not a closed-form minimax constant for general \(k\ge 2\). "Tightness without counterexamples: A new approach and new results for prophet inequalities" gives an exact minimax characterization of \(\gamma_{k,n}\) for each fixed \((k,n)\) as a semi-infinite LP over quantiles, together with a provably convergent discretization scheme that computes \(\gamma_{k,n}\) to arbitrary accuracy; it also recovers the Hill--Kertz characterization when \(k=1\). This essentially resolves the *variational* characterization and computation of \(\gamma_{n,k}\), but it still does not yield an analytic closed form for \(k\ge 2\) or identify the worst-case limiting constant as \(n\to\infty\).

Other cited papers obtain analytic formulas in restricted regimes that inform the open problem’s landscape. "Competition Versus Complexity in Multiple-Selection Prophet Inequalities" gives a genuine closed form for the best *single-threshold* policy (and a competition-complexity variant), yielding the explicit ratio \(Q_{n,k}(k/n)/k\approx 1-1/\sqrt{2\pi k}\) when \(m=n\); this is a lower bound on \(\gamma_{n,k}\) but not tight for optimal adaptive policies. "Multiunit IID Prophet Inequalities via Extreme Value Asymptotics" and "Posted Pricing and Competition in Large Markets" use extreme-value asymptotics to derive explicit \(n\to\infty\) ratios for fixed \(F\) (and often restricted policies), showing how performance depends on tail index and giving large-\(k\) expansions such as \(1-\Theta((\log k)/k)\) or \(1-\Theta(1/\sqrt{k})\) in certain classes; however, these do not perform the full minimax \(\inf_F\) over all i.i.d. distributions. Overall, the problem remains open: we now have exact LP characterizations and strong asymptotic lower bounds via nonlinear systems, plus analytic results for restricted policy classes or distribution classes, but no Hill--Kertz-style closed-form solution for the minimax \(\gamma_{n,k}\) when \(k\ge 2\).

sources.title: Tightness without counterexamples: A new approach and new results for prophet inequalities
sources.theorem_or_result: In the IID k-selection prophet inequality with n agents, the paper formulates the tight worst-case guarantee of the optimal adaptive online policy (DP) versus the prophet as a semi-infinite linear program: \(\gamma_{k,n}=\mathrm{iidLP}^{\mathrm{DP/Proph}}_{k,n}=\max\{\theta: \theta\,\mathbb{E}[\min\{\mathrm{Bin}(n,q),k\}]\le \sum_{i=1}^n\sum_{\ell=1}^k \min\{y_i^\ell,q x_i^\ell\}\ \forall q\in(0,1],\ (x,y)\in\mathcal P_n^k\}\), where \(\mathcal P_n^k\) encodes the slot-remaining state dynamics. It also gives an \(\varepsilon\)-discretization scheme (Prop. 5) to compute \(\gamma_{k,n}\) numerically to any desired accuracy, and for \(k=1\) recovers the Hill–Kertz characterization and the tight constant \(\inf_n \gamma_{1,n}=\lim_{n\to\infty}\gamma_{1,n}=\theta^*\approx 0.745\) via an ODE \(\tfrac1{\theta^*}-1=H(x)(\ln H(x)-1)-H'(x)\) with \(H(0)=1,H(1)=0\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for an analytical closed-form characterization of the worst-case optimal approximation ratio \(\gamma_{n,k}\) for IID \((k,n)\)-selection prophet inequalities for general \(k\ge2\), beyond bounds from nonlinear differential-equation systems. This paper makes substantial progress by (i) giving an exact minimax/duality-based characterization of \(\gamma_{k,n}\) for every fixed \((k,n)\) as a single semi-infinite LP over quantiles \(q\in(0,1]\), eliminating the outer infimum over IID distributions; and (ii) providing a provably accurate discretization method to compute \(\gamma_{k,n}\) numerically. However, it explicitly states it does not analytically determine \(\lim_{n\to\infty}\gamma_{k,n}\) or \(\inf_n\gamma_{k,n}\) for general \(k>1\). Thus it does not solve the open problem, but supplies a tight variational characterization and computational procedure that narrows the remaining analytic gap. The paper also notes subsequent work (Brustle et al. 2024) that reportedly derives an analytic \(\lim_{n\to\infty}\gamma_{k,n}\) using a variant framework, but that result is not contained/proved here.

sources.title: Multiunit IID Prophet Inequalities via Extreme Value Asymptotics
sources.theorem_or_result:
For distributions F on \(\mathbb{R}_+\) satisfying the extreme value condition with extreme value index \(\gamma\), the paper gives an explicit asymptotic (\(n\to\infty\)) characterization of the optimal online-to-prophet ratio for fixed \(k\):
- Define \(\mu_{n,k}=\sum_{j=0}^{k-1}\mathbb{E}[X_{n-j:n}]\) and \(V_{dp}(n,k)\) the optimal online value.
- Theorem 3.1: \(\mathrm{ACR}_k(F):=\lim_{n\to\infty} V_{dp}(n,k)/\mu_{n,k}\) equals
\[\mathrm{ACR}_k(F)=\begin{cases}\dfrac{(1-\gamma)^{1-\gamma} v_k\,\Gamma(k)}{\Gamma(k+1-\gamma)}, & \gamma\in(0,1),\\ 1, & \text{otherwise,}\end{cases}\]
where \(v_1=1\) and for \(k>1\), \(v_k-v_{k-1}\) is the unique positive solution \(x\) of \(x^{1/\gamma}+v_{k-1}x^{1/\gamma-1}-1=0\).
- Proposition 3.2: for \(\gamma\in(0,1)\), \(\mathrm{ACR}_k(F)=1-\frac{\gamma(1-\gamma)}{2}\frac{\log k}{k}+O(1/k)\), uniformly in \(k\).
- Corollary 3.3: uniformly over all such \(F\), for any \(\varepsilon>0\) and large enough \(k\), \(\mathrm{ACR}_k(F)\ge 1-\frac{\log k}{8k}(1+\varepsilon)\), and this worst case is attained at \(\gamma=1/2\) at leading order.
sources.connection:
The open problem asks for a closed-form characterization of the worst-case optimal approximation ratio \(\gamma_{n,k}=\sup_\pi\inf_F \mathrm{AR}_{n,k}(\pi,F)\) (or its worst-case limit over \(n\)) for i.i.d. \(k\)-selection, beyond implicit ODE-based bounds.

This paper does not characterize \(\inf_F\) over all i.i.d. distributions in the original (finite-\(n\)) minimax sense. Instead it fixes \(F\) and studies the instance-dependent limit \(\lim_{n\to\infty} \mathrm{CR}_{k,n}(F)\), obtaining an explicit formula in terms of the extreme value index \(\gamma\) (Theorem 3.1), and then derives an explicit large-\(k\) worst-case bound \(1-\frac{\log k}{8k}(1+\varepsilon)\) over the subclass of distributions satisfying the extreme value condition.

Thus it provides partial progress toward an analytic understanding of how the asymptotic ratio depends on \(k\), and identifies the (EVT-class) worst-case tail index \(\gamma=1/2\) at leading order, but it does not resolve the full worst-case minimax ratio \(\inf_F\) over all distributions nor give an exact closed-form for \(\gamma_{n,k}\) for general \(k\ge2\).

sources.title: The iid prophet inequality with limited flexibility
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper studies the i.i.d. single-selection prophet inequality (choose at most one value) under the restriction that the stopping rule uses at most \(k\) distinct thresholds over time (“\(k\)-dynamic” policies). It gives an exact infinite-dimensional LP characterization of the optimal worst-case ratio \(\gamma^*_{n,k}\) for this restricted policy class, proves \(\gamma^*_{n,1}=1-(1-1/n)^n\) and numerically \(\gamma^*_{n,2}\approx 0.70804\) as \(n\to\infty\). For general \(k\), via a relaxation it proves asymptotic bounds \[\gamma^*_{n,k}\ge \bar\gamma\Bigl(1-c_1(\tfrac{\log k}{k}+\tfrac{k^2}{n})\Bigr),\qquad \lim_{n\to\infty} v^*_{n,k}=v^*_{\infty,k}\le \bar\gamma\Bigl(1-c_2\tfrac{\log k}{k}\Bigr),\] where \(\bar\gamma\approx 0.745\) is the optimal fully-dynamic i.i.d. single-choice ratio (Hill–Kertz/Kertz constant).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a closed-form characterization of the optimal worst-case approximation ratio \(\gamma_{n,k}\) for the i.i.d. \(k\)-selection prophet inequality (can accept up to \(k\) items), for \(k\ge 2\). This citing paper does not study \(k\)-selection; it studies single-selection (accept at most one) but with limited flexibility in the time-varying threshold schedule (at most \(k\) distinct thresholds). Thus it does not resolve the \(k\)-selection characterization problem. Indirectly, it contributes related methodology (infinite-dimensional LP/duality, asymptotic ODE analysis) that could be relevant as tools for analyzing worst-case ratios in other i.i.d. prophet settings, but it provides neither the exact \(k\)-selection ratio nor a closed-form limit for \(\gamma_{n,k}\) in that model.

sources.title: Competition Versus Complexity in Multiple-Selection Prophet Inequalities
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper gives an exact closed-form characterization of the worst-case competitive ratio of the best single-threshold (static) algorithm when the online player sees a length-m i.i.d. sequence but is benchmarked against a prophet on length-n, selecting up to k items. Writing Y\sim\mathrm{Binomial}(m,k/n)\u0000 and Q_{m,k}(q)=\mathbb{E}[\min\{k,\mathrm{Binomial}(m,q)\}]\u0000, they prove (Theorem 1) that
\[
\gamma_{m,n,k}=\inf_F\sup_{q\in[0,1]}\frac{\mathrm{ALG}_{m,k}(F,q)}{\mathrm{OPT}_{n,k}(F)}=\frac{Q_{m,k}(k/n)}{k}=\frac{1}{k}\Bigl(k-\mathbb{E}[(k-Y)^+]\Bigr).
\]
They also characterize the resulting \((1-\varepsilon)\)-competition complexity for single-thresholds: \(\beta_1(\varepsilon)=\ln(1/\varepsilon)\) and for \(k>1\),
\[
\frac{\ln(1/\varepsilon)}{k}\le \beta_k(\varepsilon)\le 1+\frac{2\ln(1/\varepsilon)}{k-1}+\sqrt{\frac{2\ln(1/\varepsilon)}{k-1}}.
\]
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a closed-form characterization of the optimal worst-case approximation ratio \(\gamma_{n,k}=\sup_\pi\inf_F \mathrm{AR}_{n,k}(\pi,F)\) for the full i.i.d. \(k\)-selection prophet inequality (allowing optimal multi-threshold/dynamic policies) for general \(k\ge2\). This paper does not resolve that: it restricts attention to the subclass of single-threshold policies and, moreover, studies a competition-complexity variant where the online algorithm may observe \(m\ge n\) samples while the prophet sees only \(n\). Within that restricted model it *does* provide an exact analytical formula for the worst-case ratio \(\gamma_{m,n,k}\) and shows the optimal quantile is \(q=k/n\). For the original setting \(m=n\), their formula recovers the known single-threshold guarantee \(\gamma_{n,n,k}=Q_{n,k}(k/n)/k\approx 1-1/\sqrt{2\pi k}\), which is a lower bound on the true \(\gamma_{n,k}\) but not a characterization of it. Thus the contribution is partial progress: an exact characterization for a prominent restricted policy class, not for the optimal policy over all online algorithms.

sources.title: Posted Pricing and Competition in Large Markets
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper studies large-market (fixed distribution, \(n\to\infty\)) welfare guarantees of fixed-price (single-threshold) posted pricing for selling up to \(k\) identical units. For i.i.d. values from \(F\) satisfying an extreme-value condition, it defines \(\mathrm{apx}_k(F)=\liminf_{n\to\infty}\sup_T \mathbb{E}[R^{n}_{k,T}]/\sum_{j=1}^k \mathbb{E}[M_n^j]\), where \(M_n^1\ge\cdots\ge M_n^n\) are order statistics and \(R^{n}_{k,T}\) is the welfare of the fixed-price policy. Main theorem (Thm. 1):
- If \(F\) has Fr\'echet extreme type with parameter \(\alpha>1\), then for every \(k\), \(\mathrm{apx}_k(F)\ge \varphi_k(\alpha)\), where
\[\varphi_k(\alpha)=\frac{\Gamma(k)}{\Gamma(k+1-1/\alpha)}\max_{x>0} x e^{-x^{-\alpha}}\sum_{j=1}^k\sum_{s=j}^\infty \frac{x^{-s\alpha}}{s!}.\]
In particular \(\mathrm{apx}_1(F)\ge 0.712\) and \(\mathrm{apx}_k(F)\ge 1-1/\sqrt{2\pi k}\).
- If \(F\) has Gumbel or reversed-Weibull extreme type, then \(\mathrm{apx}_k(F)=1\) for all \(k\).
Tightness (Thm. 2): for Pareto(\(\alpha=2\)), \(\mathrm{apx}_k(F)\le 1-(1-\varepsilon)/\sqrt{2\pi k}\) for all large \(k\).
sources.connection:
The open problem asks for an exact (closed-form) characterization of the worst-case optimal approximation ratio \(\gamma_{n,k}=\sup_\pi\inf_F \mathrm{AR}_{n,k}(\pi,F)\) for i.i.d. \(k\)-selection prophet inequalities (general online policies), especially for \(k\ge2\). This paper does not characterize \(\gamma_{n,k}\) nor the worst-case over all i.i.d. \(F\); instead it analyzes a different regime and a restricted policy class:
- Regime: fixed \(F\), \(n\to\infty\) (“large market”), with \(F\) assumed to satisfy an extreme-value condition.
- Policies: fixed-price (single-threshold) mechanisms, not the optimal dynamic \(k\)-selection stopping rule.
Thus it does not solve the stated open problem. It is partially related because (i) it explicitly cites Br\"ustle et al. (the open-problem source) and discusses the same i.i.d. \(k\)-unit selection benchmark \(\sum_{j=1}^k \mathbb{E}[M_n^j]\), and (ii) it provides closed-form asymptotics for a restricted class of policies and distributions, showing \(\approx 1-1/\sqrt{2\pi k}\) behavior and tightness in \(k\) for Pareto tails. These results do not pin down \(\gamma_{n,k}\) but inform what can be achieved by simple thresholds in large markets and how guarantees scale with \(k\).

sources.title: Online Selection with Uncertain Disruption
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper studies a different online selection model (OS-UD) with i.i.d. values and disruption risk. It proves: - For non-adaptive single-threshold policies, the optimal worst-case competitive ratio is exactly \(1-1/e\) (Theorem 1.1). - For adaptive nonincreasing-threshold policies, \(\lim_{n\to\infty}\gamma^{AD}_{n,p,\zeta}=\theta^*\approx 0.745\) for any fixed disruption probability \(p\in(0,1)\) and recovery \(\zeta\in[0,1]\) (Theorems 1.2–1.3), where \(\theta^*\) is the Hill–Kertz constant characterized by an integral equation / ODE.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for an exact analytical characterization of the worst-case optimal approximation ratio \(\gamma_{n,k}\) for i.i.d. \(k\)-selection prophet inequalities for general \(k\ge 2\). This paper does not analyze the \(k\)-selection prophet inequality benchmark (sum of top \(k\) order statistics) nor derive \(\gamma_{n,k}\). It only references Brustle et al. (2025) as prior work introducing a nonlinear ODE system for \(k\)-prophet inequalities, and it uses the \(k=1\) Hill–Kertz equation/constant \(\theta^*\) in its own OS-UD model. Thus it provides context and a related technique (quantile/ODE methods) but no new characterization for \(\gamma_{n,k}\) when \(k\ge 2\).
